speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Solomon Awend. Today's date is November the 21st, 1995. I'm honored to conduct an interview with Mr. Henry Oertelt. The interview is being conducted in Saint Paul, Minnesota, United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Please give us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Oertelt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's spelled O-E-R-T-E-L-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No nicknames. No, except my first name was Arthur Karl Heinz. And I was always called Heinz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is January 13th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born in what city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the first memory you have as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first memory as a child, oh, there were, of course, good ones and bad ones. By that time, when I was a small child, of my grandparents, only my grandfather-- my mother's father-- was alive. And of course, I have some wonderful memories with him. He, for instance-- we-- we lived in Berlin, under fairly simple, I might say, poor circumstances. We lived in a house behind the front house, in a court-- from a courtyard. Our window faced the courtyard. And we were on the second floor. And whenever I wanted, as a small child, I wanted a penny so I could go over to the sweet shop and get myself a little piece of candy, which you could do for a penny at those days, easily. I asked my grandpa, because my mother was working. My father had died when I was about two years old, by the way. And so I asked my grandpa for a penny, hollered from the courtyard up to the window in the summertime. And he said, well, before you do that, you've got to sing a song. And so I sang some little children's song, like 'Hanschen Klein,' or something like that. And then he threw a penny down for me. And so then I went to the shop and bought something. The singing was an important thing. It was probably early training because all my family members from my mother's side were musicians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, in what part of Berlin, or in what neighborhood did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the neighborhood that we lived in Berlin was called the Wedding, which is really a laborer-- an area where the labor force mostly lived. Heavily communist-- communistic infested, so to speak, if I may say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not necessarily a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- it was not at all predominantly Jewish neighborhood. There were a few Jewish people living here and there, yes. But not-- not predominantly, no. You're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our mother was a wonderful woman. There's no question about that. She had a lot of tough luck in her life. She was a-- a dressmaker, a seamstress dressmaker. And of course, had never made much money. We really lived in very dire circumstances most of the time. As I said, my father wasn't there anymore. And so-- But she was always a happy woman. Did a lot of singing. There was a lot of singing going on in the house. And she and I and my brother, we would sing trios. And we were a very happy family. In fact, in the summertime, when the windows were open, some of the people out there would holler, more, more, more! And so we were-- we were, as I say, even though in-- in financial misery at times, we were a happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was an excellent cook. And-- and-- and a-- and a super neat housekeeper. When she scrubbed the floors, which were done at those days in Germany, there was no paint over the floor. Because the beautiful wood was to be shown in the kitchen, particularly boards. She would scrub the floor. And then she would lay out paper so it doesn't get dirty so quick again. And to wash our hands was on a steady-- steady order. And cleanliness was just-- oh, gosh. You couldn't live if you-- live with her if you were, in some instance, not so neat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, any memories of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. No members of my father at all, no. And also, not of his family. Because after he died, for some reason, we lost complete contact with his side of the family. I don't know if there were any reason. That I don't-- excuse me. And there was not much family either.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name and your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Else, as you would say in English, El-see, E-L-S-E. And my father's name was Arthur. And my middle name-- my first name turned out to be Arthur, which we never knew until we were six years-- until I was six years old. It turned out it was a mistake by the registrar at that time, who mixed up my-- my supposed-to-be first name and my father's name. I-- my mother always thought and thought she knew that my name was Karl Heinz. But then as it came to-- at one time, when I was six years old, some official matter had to be brought up. And-- and it turned out that-- that it was Arthur Karl Heinz, to her great consternation. Because in a Jewish tradition, you do not name your child after a living father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Tell me about going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to school, as-- as I got older, well, it was fine. Everything was normal. I was active. As I said, being from-- from a musicians family, I was active in singing in choirs and in small choirs, big choirs. And performances. And it was-- that's some part that made me happy. I was also in my-- at-- at my school time, even with the lower grades, very, very avid-- and I might add-- very good soccer player. And that went fine. Until it came later, when the Nazis came and took power. And I don't know if you want me to talk about this now or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll talk about that in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother is six years older than I am. And I always admired him. And we were always, always very, very close. And fortunately, to this day, fortunately, he's still around, too. And in-- thank God, in good shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Kurt, his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any, oh, expeditions or any activities or things that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my brother and I, we were avid bicycle riders. We took tours from Berlin up to the North Sea, several times, and to other areas. We-- we crossed part of the country with our bikes quite often, which was one of the main things. And of course, otherwise, he was always busy with his studies. He studied languages. And-- and I was too lazy. That was the truth. I always said, I have no time for it. But I don't know who believed me. But in any case--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Now, did you or did your family belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We belonged to a synagogue in Berlin called Ahavath Achim. It was a small, small synagogue-- synagogue, small congregation. And with a very, very stern rabbi, Rabbi Alexander. I can never forget him, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it within walking distance of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a good walking distance, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there on any kind of a regular basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, regretfully, I have to say, that when I was small, particularly, our mother was not a regular synagogue goer. Of course, we went naturally. We were-- we were mostly the three-holiday synagogue goers. Yet, nevertheless, she instilled in us, very, very early on, to be proud of the fact that we were Jewish. And always adding to it that to be proud of our Jewishness doesn't mean that we should look down upon other religions. They have their-- they have-- they should be just as proud of theirs as we are of ours. So she always preached the respect for other religions and other people to us, when we were small.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saw to it that when the time came-- like, about two years before-- before my Bar Mitzvah she saw to it that, of course, I would have my Bar Mitzvah lessons. And of course that forced me-- yes, it forced me, naturally, easily accepted-- [? so ?] to go regularly to the synagogue. It [? was a necessity ?]. Our Rabbi Alexander, you weren't-- you wouldn't dare to miss a Friday night or shabbos morning service-- shabbat morning service. Do you remember your Bar Mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, I remember my Bar Mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it for me

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my Bar Mitzvah was in a very, very simple manner. In fact, at those days, particularly in Germany-- I don't know exactly how it was here in America. But at those days, Bar Mitzvahs were never celebrated on a big level as it is nowadays. It's only usually-- usually the immediate family. And that was it. And our family was very small at that time, too. So we celebrated in-- in on our home, after we were done with the synagogue. Certainly celebrated in our home afterwards, on-- on a small level, with only the immediate, very, very small family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're 13 years old. How were conditions around the time of your Bar Mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by the time of my Bar Mitzvah, Hitler was in power already for one year. Things, by that time, became quite tight. By that time, our mother had lost her job because she was Jewish. Because Hitler had soon declared that non-Jewish employers would not be allowed to have Jews working for them. And so she lost her job. And, of course, it was very difficult for her to find another job. She did here and there on a private basis, making a dress for this person or that person, or that woman. And so it was-- it was not a very great and steady income. Times became really, really rough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do, to try and help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there really wasn't much else I could do at the time. As-- as-- as a 13-year-old, I-- I couldn't do much. I was put into apprenticeship when I was 14 years old. I'll probably talk about that later. And so there was nothing much we could do. My brother neither. My brother was, fortunately, going to school. He went by, that time, to the-- let's see, 1933. He would have been 18. He just-- He just started university, yes, for his language career. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's 1933. And times are tough. What was it like, in terms of any personal experiences that you had, where you could see a shift in the way people were treating you, treating Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started, first of all, in school, for me, personally. As I say, my mother had already had the experiences that I mentioned, and others. For me, it started in school. The first thing was that I was not allowed, suddenly, to be participating in any of the musical affairs of the school. No Jew was allowed, anymore, to sing in the choir or to participate anything else. Soon, or maybe simultaneously-- I don't know anymore exactly-- It came that we would not be allowed into other school-- to participate in other school activities like outings or museum visits and so on, so forth. We would be sent home when that took place. Pretty soon, I was thrown off the soccer team. Because I was told Jews were not allowed to be on a soccer team. And at one time, it happened that the teacher returned my homework and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --unmarked. And when I went to his desk-- when I went to his desk-- he returned the paper to-- giving him the paper to be marked. And I ask him, what-- what's happening? And he said that-- the-- I should take it back and not ask any questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so therefore, soon it came out-- I found out with the other two or three Jewish kids that were in the class-- that the teachers had the order to ignore us completely, even if we sometimes-- I had sometimes an answer to a question and got up my hand. He would completely ignore it if it was any Jewish kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So pretty soon we-- pretty soon, I must admit, I lost interest in school. Even though I realized that school is there for you to learn. But also, I think the other activities are very important. That's why they are there in the schools. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there for your own well being. So-- so I lost interest in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you mentioned before what your mother had taught you about respecting other people's religion, as well as being proud of your own. What did that make you feel like, you know, at the age of 13, when you're in somewhat of a formative stage in your life? Did you feel betrayed as-- as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if I would be able, at that age, to-- to evaluate that, very simply. I could only tell, of course, it made me very unhappy when I saw the other kids went off to their-- to their soccer playing sessions. And-- and/or the musical sessions. It certainly made me left out. And I felt like I'm thrown on a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Second class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a second-class citizen. Thrown on-- on a-- on a dung pile, practically. And so as I say, when I eventually-- and sort of I might as well finish the school business there. When eventually I was thrown out of school at age 14, when the principal called me and said that I should go home, pack my stuff, that Jewish kids are not allowed to be in public school anymore. So I was not too unhappy at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that have any impact on your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother-- not at that moment. My brother was at the university-- and at the university and studied his languages. And he was really brilliant. He speaks nine languages. And he was one of the very last Jewish students on the university that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that were allowed to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which university?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Berliner university, the Berlin University. At that-- I don't know if there was any other name involved in the-- at this moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: November 10, do you remember what happened that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, the night of November 9th to 10th, to the 10th, was the famous or infamous Kristallnacht, The Night of Broken Glass, as it is know, or Crystal Night. That was the night where, during that night, almost all synagogues in Germany, as well as in Austria-- which by that time had been annexed by Hitler-- were destroyed by fire or explosions, or what have you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, do you remember-- do you-- what-- what do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember that we were ready to go to bed the night of the ninth. Of course, first of all, let me go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It started on November 8th, when a-- a young Jewish student, living with his uncle in Paris, a-- a-- a German from Polish-- of Polish descent, a young Jewish student, spending some time with his uncle in Paris, going there to school. When on that day, he went to-- into the Embassy and shot, point blank, one of the German officials, a fellow by the name of vom Rath. He did that. And as he hollered out, this is to pay you back for what you did to my family and the other Jewish people. It happened to be that he found out that his father was badly, badly beaten when Hitler ordered the transportation of former Polish Jews back into Poland. And so he went and shot this guy. He was, of course, immediately taken into custody-- custody. But immediately, at that time the next morning in-- in Berlin, 100 Jewish men were rounded up at random, put against the wall, and shot. And Hitler announced that, if this man-- vom Rath-- will die, the Jews-- there will be something happening to the Jews which they have never had-- experienced. And so, frankly, it turned out that-- just imagine that Jews started praying for the well being of a super Nazi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you hear this on the radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: What'd-- what'd that make you feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and newspaper headlines. It made us feel a little frightened. And while we, in a way, were happy that somebody did something, you know, stood up, on the other hand, we were a little frightened of what would happen. Because now so-- by now, so many bad things had happened to Jews. Jews had lost their jobs. Jews were beaten up wherever-- wherever you saw, they're up and down the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe one of the incidences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- a Jewish couple was put out of their little shop. And-- or-- or had been standing outside of their shop. And these brown-shirted hoodlums, the SR, would-- came marching by and started to holler and yell at them and spit at them. And so maybe these people answered something. I don't know. I wasn't that close to it at that moment. But before you knew it, they were just beat-- the-- the brownshirts would beat up on them. And people started to mill around. Some of them would holler encouraging words. There were also some people that stood on the sidelines, shaking their heads in disbelief and would sometimes, when it happened again, would have-- sometimes mutter, well, this isn't right. We know Hitler doesn't like the Jews. But he always said he's a man at law-- a man of law and order. Just give it some time till he's more in the saddle. He was just starting out in-- in his new regime. He will not allow that. And so the police would never step in. And this-- this appeared, happened more and more often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any parades or any marching down the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. There were-- I remember a very vivid scene when I probably was-- I don't know exactly how old I was. Maybe five, six, or seven years old when I was of the age that I was fully aware of the fact that I'm a Jewish child. My mother and I walked down the streets somewhere. And these brown-shirted characters, a hoard-- a big hoard of those characters came down the street, marching, in marching formation, with a little band in front of them. And singing a constantly-repeating refrain to-- to a regular song. And that refrain would come up again and again. Until, finally, it even landed into my little child's ear. And-- and-- and I would-- I will like-- I'll recite it in in exact translation, and hear, well, that constantly reappearing ditty. And it goes like, once the blood of the Jews squirts off our knives, everything will go twice as well. And the little child that I was, blood of the Jews, that stuck in, and the knife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember very vividly, I-- when I turned to my mother. I said, Mom, what do they mean? What do they mean? Do they-- blood of the Jews off their knives? What do they mean? Do they want to kill us all? What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still feel my mother's hand on my head when she says, listen, honey. Don't worry about it. These are a bunch of loud mouths. They like to do that. It doesn't mean anything. Just-- just forget it. Well, you couldn't forget it because you could hear these-- this and other songs of that nature, by the way--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --constantly, whenever these-- these hoodlums marched by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Kristallnacht. You went to sleep, what, on the ninth, that evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Yeah, on the ninth, that evening. So after this all had been announced-- and, by the way, the-- the-- the reports came constantly on this guy-- on this guy's condition, which was deteriorating very fast. And as I said, I believe that the miracle happened that the Jews were praying for some super Nazi to survive. Because we were afraid if this guy dies, there really might be something. We couldn't figure out what. And on the evening of the ninth, it had been during-- on the afternoon of the ninth, it had been announced that this man has succumbed to his wounds and died. And nothing much took place really. But among the Jews came the warning out, don't go on the streets if you don't absolutely have to. Stay at home and do nothing. And so we were waiting for what happened. You know, like the second shoe to fall. And we went to bed probably something like 11:00-ish or so. My mother draws the curtains for the night. And she says, my gosh. She says, look guys, to my brother and me. The sky looks really red out there. What-- I wonder what's going on there. There must be a fire somewhere. But we didn't hear any sirens or nothing. And so, you know, every once in a while somewhere there's a fire in the big city. And so we didn't think much. We went to bed. Until the next morning, when I was on my way to work, to my apprenticeship. When I was-- I had my bicycle. And all of a sudden, a street away, I went by. There was-- there were a couple of Jewish stores. And all of a sudden, I noticed that all their window glass was broken out on the street. And some of the items were still scattered around on the street from the ransacking. Then I came by, with my bicycle also, by the synagogue in the neighborhood. And that was burned down. And at the moment, I couldn't figure out what has happened. And-- but soon, of-- and I had to carry my bike. There was so much glass on the streets. I had to-- I didn't dare to put the-- not to talk about riding, to push anymore with the glass. So I had to carry my bike for a block or two, or something like that, in order not to cut the tires. And then, of course, it turned out to be that during that night, as-- as Hitler, in the headlines, claimed 'spontaneous reaction by our German population against the dastardly crime committed by-- by the organized Jewry of the world,' and so on, so forth. They pushed this kind of thing like that out. Propaganda was always a big thing, of course. And so-- and so then I found out that all the synagogues have-- during that night, also, about 35 Jewish men were killed. Among them rabbis that tried to defend their synagogues, as well as about-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --30,000 Jewish men were rounded up during that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Did you talk to any of your friends or any other Jewish people about what happened, or any reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that moment I went on, then, to my-- to my workplace, of course. And there-- there we talked a little bit about that there was another couple of Jewish apprentices where I was too. And so we talked about that. And well, we were wondering what happened. We couldn't put together, at this point. Then later on, of course, next day or so, then we knew what happened all over the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after Kristallnacht that started to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Kristallnacht is considered to be the beginning of the Holocaust. A prelude, as it were, of the Holocaust. From that time on, out of Germany, regularly, Jews were picked up during the nights and were sent to the concentration camps. You see, Hitler built already, the first concentration camps-- camp only two months after he was in power. Dachau, near Munich. And then from then on, there-- they grew up like mushrooms, all over the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody that you knew that was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, pretty soon friends disappeared. And family members disappeared. Neighbors disappeared. And it was interesting. In-- in our building that we lived, there were a couple of other Jewish families. Nobody ever asked what happened to these or what happened to those. And so people sort of accepted it or didn't dare to-- to-- to ask or talk about it. Because of course, pretty soon this state, Hitler's state had developed into a dictatorship, particularly due to the fact that all other political parties-- and they were up to 30 at times-- were outlawed, were not allowed to operate. And Hitler rounded them up if they didn't believe it, and would send them to the concentration camp. That was how Dachau was built in the first place, to-- for the opponents of the Reich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you personally after Kristallnacht, that started to change for you and for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, things-- not specifically after Kristallnacht. Things had-- had-- had happened before that already. But number one, I didn't mention that when I was 14 years old, after I had to leave school, I was fortunate enough to-- taken in by some man who-- who taught me the building-- the designing and building of furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in an apprenticeship. And four years afterwards, in 1939, I had a chance to finish it yet. I became a furniture designer and furniture-- and furniture builder. And so this was, first of all my-- that's when, I said, with my bicycle I went to the workplace. That was my workplace that I referred to earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Things started to get tighter before the Kristallnacht already. In other words, the thumb screws were tightened little by little. In other words, in my lectures that I am holding often, I'm calling this the process of dehumanization. Which started to take place little by little. You know, from the time on, in my school and so on. And as I say, by that time, Jewish people had lost their jobs all over the places. The unemployment among Jewish people was just horrendous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So pretty soon, the order was given that Jews were not allowed to go to any public places. No restaurants, no movie-- no theaters, no concerts, my beloved soccer games. Nowhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to go anyplace anymore. A curfew was declared, that Jews had to be home in the winter, at 8 o'clock. And in the summer, generously, at 9 o'clock in the evening. And so there was not much going on. It had the-- the-- the wonderful advantage that you became closer with your friends. We got to meet more with our friends circles, friendship circles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about some of your friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my friends were mostly musicians. And so we had a musicians group sometimes of, maybe, eight people around. And we would do-- having fun with doing-- having, actually, musical jam sessions. Many of them played instruments-- instruments. We did a lot of singing. And, of course, we also were admiring, at those days, the records of Louis Armstrong and Ella Fitzgerald, and all the others that were-- that were prohibited to have in Germany. If you were caught with records of these people, then you were-- you were picked up. And you were never seen again, even for this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Tape-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were not really much scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Change.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
